annotations.text: Sustained monomorphic ventricular tachycardia can be seen in patients with virtually any disease affecting the myocardium and can even be seen infrequently in the absence of overt structural heart disease.
The presence of sustained monomorphic ventricular tachycardia is often associated with a poor outcome in patients with heart disease.

One syndrome that deserves mention in this case is arrhythmogenic right ventricular dysplasia, which is primarily a right ventricular cardiomyopathy, frequently with little or no left ventricular involvement.
Although this patient seems to have had disproportionate right ventricular involvement, other clinical findings would not be explained by this diagnosis.
In this case ventricular tachycardia was occurring in the setting of a global cardiomyopathy or myocarditis.
Cardiomyopathies comprise dilated, hypertrophic, and restrictive types.
The characteristic echocardiographic findings include increased wall thickness without chamber enlargement and evidence of abnormal diastolic function with ultimate systolic dysfunction.
In view of the presence of thoracic lymphadenopathy in this young nonsmoker, the possibility of lymphoma should be considered.
Cardiac involvement, which is found at autopsy in up to 24 percent of patients with lymphoma, is typically seen in the form of extension to the parietal pericardium, with or without effusions.
Diffuse myocardial involvement is uncommon and is usually not associated with clinical manifestations.
A presentation with overt heart disease is rare and generally followed by a fulminant course because of the association with high-grade tumors.
The diagnosis of sarcoidosis should be strongly considered in any young patient with conduction-system disease and ventricular tachycardia.
Sarcoidosis is a systemic granulomatous disorder of unknown cause characterized by noncaseating epithelioid-cell granulomas involving multiple organ systems.
The most frequent presentation of sarcoidosis is in young adults with bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy, pulmonary infiltrates, and skin or eye lesions.
Generalized cutaneous anergy occurs in more than two thirds of the patients.
Granulomatous involvement of the heart can cause either a restrictive or a dilated cardiomyopathy.
Pulmonary involvement in sarcoidosis is typically bilateral with four radiographic patterns.
The presence of infiltrates without lymphadenopathy may reflect a later stage of the disease.
Upper-lobe involvement simulating pulmonary tuberculosis was found in 54 of 616 patients (9 percent) at one institution and was often unilateral.
The predominant right-upper-lobe reticulonodular infiltrate and lymphadenopathy in this patient were consistent with pulmonary sarcoidosis.
Although 20 to 25 percent of patients with sarcoidosis have cardiac involvement at autopsy, only 5 percent of them have clinically overt disease.
Most of the patients with confirmed cardiac involvement die suddenly.
Autopsies demonstrated a propensity for involvement of the left ventricular free wall and the base of the interventricular septum.
This distribution of granulomas may explain the conduction-system disturbances that occur in up to half the patients.
Localized involvement of the conduction system can lead to clinically important consequences without extensive myocardial damage.
Extensive myocardial involvement can also occur with few extracardiac symptoms and may be correlated with sudden cardiac death, although this point is disputed.

Percutaneous endomyocardial biopsy is diagnostic in less than 30 percent of patients with cardiac sarcoidosis, probably because it is difficult to perform a percutaneous biopsy of the base of the septum and left ventricular free wall, and the lesions have a patchy distribution.
Frequent clinical manifestations include conduction abnormalities and ventricular tachyarrhythmias.
Conduction-system disease ranges from first-degree atrioventricular block to complete atrioventricular block.
Right-bundle-branch block is a common manifestation of disease below the bundle of His.
Ventricular tachyarrhythmias are often refractory to treatment.

The presentation may also include myocarditis with congestive heart failure, sudden cardiac death, supraventricular tachyarrhythmia, mitral-valve insufficiency due to papillary-muscle dysfunction, formation of a left ventricular aneurysm, acute myocardial infarction, and pericarditis.
Congestive heart failure can be seen in either acute or chronic disease.
The formation of ventricular aneurysm may be promoted by steroid treatment.
A substantial number of patients with cardiac sarcoidosis do not have signs of extracardiac involvement.
In one report of seven patients with ventricular tachycardia complicating sarcoidosis, six had extracardiac disease.
Although the mean (±SD) left ventricular ejection fraction in this group was reduced (34±8 percent), two patients had only minimal wall-motion abnormalities.
Four patients had right ventricular involvement, and five had mitral insufficiency.
None of the four patients who underwent endomyocardial biopsy were found to have granulomas, which is consistent with the findings in other reports.
Steroid treatment did not decrease the frequency of recurrent ventricular tachycardia, and arrhythmias in this group of patients were refractory to treatment.
Implantable-defibrillator therapy was necessary in most of the patients.
I believe that this patient had systemic sarcoidosis involving the heart and lungs.
Generalized cutaneous anergy is typical of sarcoidosis.
The pattern of lung involvement, although atypical, is consistent with pulmonary sarcoidosis.
Finally, the cardiomyopathy, conduction-system dysfunction, and sustained ventricular tachycardia are common manifestations of cardiac sarcoidosis.
The diagnostic procedure was probably a transbronchial biopsy at the time of bronchoscopic examination.
Since previous myocardial biopsies had been nondiagnostic, a repeated myocardial biopsy, even in the light of the progressive left ventricular dysfunction, would not have been my first choice.
